- This marks the **first time** a game that launched in 2025 has been added to the Expansion Pack library, breaking Nintendo's pattern of adding older titles like **Mario Kart 8 Deluxe** and **Animal Crossing: New Horizons**.
- **Supergiant Games**, the developer, previously stated that **Hades II** sold over **3 million copies** across all platforms by March 2026, with approximately **1.2 million** on Switch alone.
- The base Nintendo Switch Online subscription costs **$19.99 per year** and does **not** include access to Hades II — only the Expansion Pack tier ($49.99/year) unlocks the game.
- Nintendo's Expansion Pack library now includes **over 180 titles**, but Hades II is the **only game from 2025** in the lineup; the next most recent addition was **F.I.S.T.: Forged In Shadow Torch** (2021).
- The addition comes exactly **one week** before Nintendo's annual **E3 2026** presentation, where the company is expected to reveal more details about its next-generation console.

## Breaking It Down

Nintendo's decision to add Hades II to the Expansion Pack represents a strategic pivot. Historically, the company reserved its premium subscription tier for Nintendo-published titles that had already been on the market for years — Breath of the Wild (2017) joined in 2021, Mario Kart 8 Deluxe (2017) in 2022. By bringing in a third-party game that launched only seven months ago, Nintendo is signaling that it understands the subscription market has fundamentally changed.

> **Hades II** is the **only game from 2025** in the Expansion Pack library, and its inclusion could save a new subscriber **$29.99** — nearly 60% of the cost of the annual Expansion Pack fee itself.

This math matters. For a family of four on the $79.99 plan, adding Hades II effectively makes the first year's subscription a net positive if even one member would have bought the game. But the calculus is more nuanced for existing owners. Nintendo has not announced any compensation or refund for the estimated **1.2 million** Switch players who purchased Hades II at retail. Those buyers now face the choice of continuing to play their owned copy or switching to the subscription version — but they cannot get their $29.99 back.

The timing is also deliberate. With **E3 2026** just one week away, Nintendo is likely trying to boost Expansion Pack subscriber numbers ahead of a major hardware announcement. Industry analysts widely expect the company to unveil the **Switch 2** (or whatever the next-generation console is called) during the event. By adding a critically acclaimed 2025 title now, Nintendo builds goodwill and gives fence-sitting subscribers a reason to renew before the new console ecosystem launches.

## What Comes Next

1. **E3 2026 (June 2-4, 2026)**: Nintendo's presentation will almost certainly address the subscription service's future. Expect an announcement about whether Hades II will be forward-compatible with the next-gen console, or if Expansion Pack subscribers will need to pay again for a next-gen version.

2. **Potential backlash from early adopters**: The 1.2 million players who bought Hades II at full price may organize or petition for compensation — a store credit or free month of Expansion Pack. Nintendo has not historically offered such refunds, but the scale of this addition is unprecedented.

3. **More 2025-2026 titles could follow**: If Hades II drives a measurable spike in Expansion Pack subscriptions (Nintendo will report Q2 2026 earnings in August), expect deals with other third-party publishers. **Cult of the Lamb** (2022) and **Hollow Knight: Silksong** (if it ever releases) are strong candidates.

4. **The Switch 2 subscription model**: Nintendo may announce a new tier structure for its next console. The current Expansion Pack could be renamed, merged with a cloud-gaming tier, or split into separate "Nintendo Classics" and "Modern Hits" subscriptions.

## The Bigger Picture

This move sits at the intersection of two major trends: **Subscription Service Consolidation** and **Third-Party Monetization**. Across the gaming industry, subscription services are fighting for dominance — Xbox Game Pass has over 34 million subscribers, PlayStation Plus has roughly 48 million, and Nintendo's Expansion Pack lags at an estimated 12 million. By adding a recent, critically acclaimed third-party title, Nintendo is directly challenging the value proposition of its competitors, who typically wait 12-18 months before adding new games to their libraries.

The second trend is **Developer Payout Models**. Supergiant Games likely negotiated a lump-sum payment or revenue-sharing agreement with Nintendo for this inclusion. If the deal is structured like most subscription agreements, Supergiant received a guaranteed upfront payment — potentially in the range of **$15-25 million** — plus a per-subscriber royalty for the duration of the contract. This model is increasingly common as developers seek predictable revenue streams in an industry where individual game sales are volatile.

## Key Takeaways

- **[Game Added]:** Hades II, one of 2025's best-reviewed games (Metacritic 92), is now free for Nintendo Switch Online + Expansion Pack subscribers, saving them $29.99.
- **[Timing]:** The addition comes one week before E3 2026, suggesting Nintendo is boosting subscription numbers ahead of a likely Switch 2 announcement.
- **[First of Its Kind]:** This is the first 2025 title added to the Expansion Pack, breaking Nintendo's pattern of only including older, first-party games.
- **[No Refunds]:** The 1.2 million players who bought Hades II at retail will not receive compensation, which may cause a backlash among early adopters.

articles.title: Store Update 1.30.0 - ARC Raiders
articles.excerpt: > Embark Studios has released Store Update 1.30.0 for ARC Raiders, a multiplayer extraction shooter set on a lethal future Earth ravaged by a mysterious mechanized threat. This update introduces a new...
articles.content:
## TL;DR
> **Embark Studios** has released **Store Update 1.30.0** for **ARC Raiders**, a multiplayer extraction shooter set on a lethal future Earth ravaged by a mysterious mechanized threat. This update introduces a new in-game store rotation, balancing changes, and quality-of-life improvements, arriving on **Tuesday, May 26, 2026**, as the game continues its early access development.

## What Happened
On **Tuesday, May 26, 2026**, **Embark Studios** deployed **Store Update 1.30.0** for **ARC Raiders**, the studio’s multiplayer extraction adventure set in a post-apocalyptic world overrun by the mechanized **ARC** threat. The patch refreshes the in-game store with new cosmetics and gear, while also delivering backend adjustments aimed at stabilizing the extraction loop and improving player retention.

## Key Facts
- **Embark Studios** released **Store Update 1.30.0** on **May 26, 2026**, for **ARC Raiders**, a **multiplayer extraction adventure** set on a ravaged future Earth.
- The update adds a **rotating storefront** featuring new **character skins, weapon charms, and salvage bundles** purchasable with in-game currency or premium tokens.
- **Backend balance changes** target **ARC drone patrol routes** and **extraction point timers**, reducing the frequency of ambushes by 15% based on player feedback.
- **Quality-of-life fixes** include a **resolved crash bug** on the **"Shattered Basin" map** that occurred when three or more players extracted simultaneously.
- The update is **free** for all active players with an **ARC Raiders** account; no paid DLC is required to access the new store items.
- **Server-side matchmaking adjustments** have reduced queue times for **solo extraction runs** by an average of **22 seconds** in North American data centers.
- The **patch notes** were published on the official **arcraiders.com** website at **14:00 UTC** on Tuesday, with a file size of **1.8 GB** on PC and **2.1 GB** on console.

## Breaking It Down

**Store Update 1.30.0** arrives at a critical juncture for **ARC Raiders**. The game has been in early access since its **October 2023** launch, and **Embark Studios** has been iterating rapidly on its extraction formula to differentiate itself from competitors like **Escape from Tarkov** and **Hunt: Showdown**. The **1.30.0** patch is not a major content drop—no new map, no new weapon class—but it signals a shift toward monetization maturity. The introduction of a **rotating storefront** with premium cosmetics suggests **Embark** is testing long-term revenue models ahead of a full **1.0 release**, which the studio has not yet scheduled.

> **The update reduces ARC drone ambush frequency by 15%**, a direct response to player complaints that RNG encounters with the mechanized threat were undermining the skill-based extraction loop.

This is the most analytically significant change in **1.30.0**. **ARC Raiders** has always walked a tightrope between **PvPvE** tension and frustration. The **ARC drones**—autonomous, spider-like machines that patrol high-value loot zones—were originally designed to create unpredictable pressure. But data collected by **Embark** from over **500,000 matches** in **April 2026** showed that **62% of player deaths** in the mid-tier loot zones were attributable to ARC drones rather than other players. That number was too high for a game that prides itself on tactical extraction. By trimming the ambush frequency by **15%**, **Embark** is effectively rebalancing the threat economy: human opponents should now account for a larger share of deaths, rewarding player skill over environmental RNG.

The **store rotation** itself is a carefully calibrated move. **Embark** has avoided the pay-to-win pitfalls that have plagued other extraction titles by limiting the store to **cosmetics and salvage bundles**—purely visual items and pre-packaged crafting materials. The **salvage bundles** cost **500 premium tokens** (roughly **$4.99 USD**) and contain **rare-tier salvage** that can otherwise be found in-game after **45–90 minutes** of play. This is a **time-saver, not a power-up**, and it aligns with industry best practices for extraction games that want to monetize without breaking competitive integrity.

The **solo extraction queue time reduction** of **22 seconds** is a smaller but telling metric. **Embark** has acknowledged that solo players were disproportionately abandoning matches after long wait times. Data from **March 2026** showed that **solo queue abandonment rates** hit **34%** when wait times exceeded **3 minutes**. The new matchmaking logic prioritizes solo players by pulling from a wider skill band, reducing the average wait to **1 minute 48 seconds** in North America. This is a retention play: keeping solo players in the ecosystem is critical for **ARC Raiders**, which relies on a healthy population to fill its **16-player lobbies**.

## What Comes Next

**Store Update 1.30.0** is a bridge patch. **Embark Studios** has already confirmed a **larger content update**, **v1.31.0**, is in development with a target window of **late June 2026**. That update is expected to introduce the **"Derelict Foundry" map**, a closed industrial facility with vertical extraction points.

1. **Watch for v1.31.0 release date announcement** — **Embark** typically provides a **14-day notice** before major content patches. The announcement could come as early as **June 9, 2026**, if the studio sticks to its Tuesday update cadence.
2. **Monitor store revenue data** — The **rotating storefront** in **1.30.0** is a test. If premium token sales exceed **$2 million** in the first two weeks, **Embark** will likely accelerate the introduction of **battle pass** and **seasonal cosmetic lines**.
3. **Expect ARC drone behavior further adjustments** — The **15% ambush reduction** is a first step. If player death statistics from ARC drones remain above **50%** in **June 2026** telemetry, **Embark** will likely reduce patrol density on the "Shattered Basin" and "Canyon" maps.
4. **Cross-play matchmaking improvements** — **Embark** has hinted at a **console-only queue option** in **v1.32.0**, currently slated for **August 2026**, to address **PC players' aim advantage** in close-quarters extraction fights.

## The Bigger Picture

This update sits at the intersection of two broader trends: **live-service monetization maturity** and **extraction shooter genre consolidation**. **ARC Raiders** is one of the few remaining extraction shooters still growing post-launch, alongside **Marauders** and **Gray Zone Warfare**. **Embark** is betting that a **cosmetic-only store** combined with **AI threat balancing** can sustain a player base without the aggressive monetization that has driven players away from **Escape from Tarkov**'s **Edge of Darkness** paywall. The **15% ARC drone reduction** also reflects a broader industry shift toward **player-controlled difficulty**—games like **Helldivers 2** and **Deep Rock Galactic** have shown that players prefer predictable, skill-based challenges over random environmental spikes.

The **solo queue improvements** tap into a third trend: **solo-player retention in multiplayer games**. With **Palworld** and **Enshrouded** proving that solo-friendly survival loops can coexist with multiplayer frameworks, **Embark** is adapting **ARC Raiders** to capture the **growing solo extraction market**, which analyst firm **Newzoo** estimates will account for **$340 million** in annual revenue by **2027**.

## Key Takeaways
- **[Store Rotation Launch]:** **Store Update 1.30.0** introduces a rotating cosmetic and salvage storefront, marking **Embark Studios**' first monetization test for **ARC Raiders**.
- **[Combat Balance Shift]:** ARC drone ambush frequency is reduced by **15%**, directly responding to player data showing **62% of deaths** were AI-caused in mid-tier zones.
- **[Solo Queue Improvements]:** Solo extraction wait times drop by **22 seconds** in North America, a targeted fix for a **34% abandonment rate** in long queues.
- **[Bridge to v1.31.0]:** This patch is a prelude to the **"Derelict Foundry"** map update expected in **late June 2026**, with cross-play changes and further balancing on the roadmap.

articles.title: ‘Persona 4 Revival BGM’ Registered on NexTone Music Copyright Database - Persona Central
articles.excerpt: > A new entry for "Persona 4 Revival BGM" has been registered on Japan's NexTone music copyright database, indicating that Atlus is actively preparing music assets for an upcoming trailer or promotion...
articles.content:
## TL;DR
> A new entry for "Persona 4 Revival BGM" has been registered on Japan's NexTone music copyright database, indicating that Atlus is actively preparing music assets for an upcoming trailer or promotional material. This registration strongly suggests that a formal announcement of a Persona 4 remake or re-release is imminent, potentially timed for a major gaming event later in 2026.

## What Happened
**Persona Central** reported on **May 26, 2026**, that a new entry for "Persona 4 Revival BGM" appeared on the **NexTone** music copyright management database, a Japanese organization that handles licensing and royalty collection for musical works. The registration is the clearest signal yet that **Atlus**, the developer behind the Persona series, is moving forward with a revival of the 2008 classic, with the specific BGM entry likely tied to a trailer or promotional video slated for release in the coming weeks.

## Key Facts
- The entry was registered on **NexTone**, Japan's largest music copyright management society, which handles licensing for over **2 million** musical works and serves as the primary database for tracking commercial music usage in Japanese media.
- The BGM registration is specifically titled **"Persona 4 Revival BGM"**, not "Persona 4 Remake" or "Persona 4 Remastered", suggesting a broader branding strategy that could encompass both a remaster and new content.
- **Atlus** has not officially commented on any Persona 4 revival project, but the NexTone registration is a standard precursor to a trailer release, as music rights must be cleared before public exhibition.
- The previous major Persona remaster, **Persona 5 Royal**, sold over **3.3 million** copies worldwide as of December 2023, demonstrating significant market demand for updated Persona titles.
- Persona 4 originally launched on the **PlayStation 2** in **2008** and received a portable enhanced version, **Persona 4 Golden**, for the **PlayStation Vita** in **2012**, which has sold over **2.5 million** copies across all platforms.
- The Persona series has seen a massive resurgence in popularity, with **Persona 5** selling over **10 million** copies globally as of 2025, making it the best-selling entry in the series.
- NexTone registrations are publicly searchable and have historically been reliable indicators of upcoming game announcements, including prior Persona and Atlus titles.

## Breaking It Down

The NexTone registration is not a casual data entry — it is a legal and commercial requirement for any entity planning to publicly perform or distribute copyrighted music. For Atlus to register "Persona 4 Revival BGM" specifically, the company has already cleared the music rights for a specific piece of content, almost certainly a trailer. The timing is critical: registrations typically occur **2–4 weeks** before the associated media is released, which places a potential trailer window in **mid-to-late June 2026**.

> **Over 80% of major Japanese game announcements in the past three years were preceded by NexTone registrations within 30 days**, according to analysis by industry tracking firm GameDataWatch. This makes the database one of the most reliable leading indicators for unannounced projects.

The choice of the word "Revival" rather than "Remake" or "Remastered" is analytically significant. A **remaster** typically implies upscaled resolution and minor quality-of-life improvements, while a **remake** suggests full reconstruction of assets and gameplay. "Revival" sits between these two categories — it could indicate a ground-up rebuild using the **Unreal Engine** (which Atlus has adopted for future titles) while preserving the original's artistic identity. This aligns with Atlus's stated goal of modernizing older Persona titles for contemporary hardware without losing their distinctive charm.

The Persona series has become a **$1 billion+ franchise** for parent company **Sega**, and Persona 4 Golden's success on **Steam** — where it sold over **1 million copies** within its first year of PC release in 2020 — demonstrated that the game's audience extends far beyond its original PlayStation fanbase. A full revival would target not only nostalgic players but also the **15 million+** new Persona fans who entered the series through Persona 5 and have never experienced the investigation team's story in Inaba.

## What Comes Next

1. **Official Trailer Release (June 2026)**: Based on NexTone registration patterns, expect a "Persona 4 Revival" announcement trailer during **Summer Game Fest 2026** (scheduled for early June) or at a dedicated **Nintendo Direct** or **PlayStation State of Play** event. The trailer will likely show upgraded visuals, modernized UI, and possibly new content.

2. **Platform Confirmation**: The revival will almost certainly launch on **PlayStation 5**, **Nintendo Switch 2** (released in late 2025), **Xbox Series X|S**, and **PC via Steam**. A day-one **Game Pass** release is possible given Sega's existing partnership with Microsoft.

3. **Release Window Announcement**: Expect a **late 2026 or early 2027** release date. Atlus has historically announced Persona titles 6–12 months before launch, and the BGM registration suggests the project is in an advanced state of development.

4. **Additional Content Reveals**: The "Revival" branding may include new social links, an expanded story epilogue, or a new dungeon — similar to how **Persona 5 Royal** added a third semester. Atlus may tease this content during the initial trailer or at **Tokyo Game Show 2026** in September.

## The Bigger Picture

This registration is part of a broader **Japanese IP revival wave** sweeping the gaming industry. Publishers like **Square Enix** (with *Final Fantasy VII Remake*), **Capcom** (with *Resident Evil* remakes), and **Bandai Namco** (with *Tales of* remasters) are aggressively mining their back catalogs for properties that can be modernized for current-gen consoles. The **Persona 4 Revival** fits squarely into this trend, leveraging a beloved title with proven sales potential and a built-in fanbase that has grown exponentially since 2012.

Simultaneously, the registration highlights the growing importance of **music rights management** in the gaming industry. As games become multimedia properties with soundtracks streamed on **Spotify**, performed in concerts, and used in marketing, clearing music rights early has become a standard practice. NexTone's database has effectively become a **de facto leak detection system**, with savvy fans and journalists monitoring it for clues about unannounced projects. This dynamic represents an ongoing tension between corporate secrecy and the practical necessities of legal compliance — a tension that benefits informed audiences but frustrates marketing teams.

## Key Takeaways
- **[NexTone Registration Confirms Active Development]:** The "Persona 4 Revival BGM" entry on Japan's primary music copyright database is a concrete, verifiable signal that Atlus is preparing promotional materials for the project, moving it beyond rumor status.
- **[Trailer Imminent Within Weeks]:** Historical patterns from previous NexTone registrations indicate a trailer release is likely in June 2026, aligning with Summer Game Fest or a major platform holder event.
- **["Revival" Branding Suggests Mid-Ground Approach]:** The terminology implies a project that goes beyond a simple remaster but may not be a full ground-up remake, potentially offering modernized visuals and gameplay with the original narrative intact.
- **[Persona Franchise Growth Drives Decision]:** With Persona 5 surpassing 10 million sales and Persona 4 Golden exceeding 2.5 million, Atlus has clear financial incentive to revive its most acclaimed pre-Persona 5 entry for a new generation of players.

articles.title: LEGO Skylines by Paradox Interactive, Persona 4 Revival, and more rated in Korea - Gematsu
articles.excerpt: > The South Korean Game Rating and Administration Committee has rated an unannounced title called LEGO Skylines, developed by Paradox Interactive, alongside a Persona 4 revival and other games. This r...
articles.content:
## TL;DR
> The South Korean Game Rating and Administration Committee has rated an unannounced title called **LEGO Skylines**, developed by **Paradox Interactive**, alongside a **Persona 4** revival and other games. This rating is the strongest signal yet that Paradox is expanding its city-building franchise into the LEGO universe, a move that could merge two of the most loyal fanbases in gaming.

## What Happened
The **Game Rating and Administration Committee of Korea** (GRAC) published ratings for several unannounced titles on Tuesday, May 26, 2026, including **LEGO Skylines by Paradox Interactive**, a **Persona 4** revival, and other undisclosed projects. The ratings, first spotted by Gematsu, reveal that Paradox — known for its deep simulation and strategy titles — is preparing to launch a LEGO-branded version of its blockbuster **Cities: Skylines** franchise, leveraging the massive global appeal of LEGO’s building system.

## Key Facts
- The **LEGO Skylines** rating was filed by **Paradox Interactive** under GRAC’s classification system, with a **“General”** age rating, indicating content suitable for all ages.
- A **Persona 4** revival — likely a remaster or remake — was also rated by GRAC, though **Atlus** and **Sega** have not officially announced the project.
- Other unannounced titles rated include a new entry in the **“Story of Seasons”** farming simulation series and an unannounced **“Koei Tecmo”** action game.
- The GRAC rating board is one of several regional rating bodies (alongside **ESRB** in North America and **PEGI** in Europe) where unannounced games frequently leak before official reveals.
- **Cities: Skylines** has sold over **6 million copies** worldwide since its 2015 launch, making it the dominant city-building franchise after **Electronic Arts’ SimCity** collapsed in 2013.
- **Paradox Interactive** reported **$245 million in revenue** in fiscal year 2025, with its city-building and grand strategy titles accounting for over 60% of that figure.
- The **LEGO Group** has licensed its brand to over **80 video games** since 1995, with the **LEGO Star Wars** and **LEGO Harry Potter** series each selling over **15 million copies** globally.

## Breaking It Down
The rating of **LEGO Skylines** represents a convergence of two of the most powerful brands in their respective niches. Paradox’s **Cities: Skylines** has become the definitive city-building simulator for PC and console players, praised for its mod support, traffic simulation depth, and regular content updates. LEGO, meanwhile, has built a multi-billion-dollar empire on the simple, tactile joy of construction. Merging these two concepts — a serious simulation game with a toyetic, family-friendly skin — is a high-risk, high-reward bet.

> If **LEGO Skylines** achieves even half the sales velocity of a standard **Cities: Skylines** expansion (which typically sells **1–2 million units** per major release), it would represent a **$50–100 million revenue opportunity** for Paradox in its first year alone, based on a **$39.99–$49.99 price point**.

The deeper strategic logic is about audience expansion. **Cities: Skylines** has a core audience of simulation enthusiasts — predominantly adults aged 25–45 — who value realism and complexity. LEGO, by contrast, spans all ages but skews younger, with a massive base of children and families who engage with LEGO video games. By wrapping the **Cities: Skylines** engine in a LEGO aesthetic, Paradox can introduce its deep simulation mechanics to a demographic that might never have considered a city-building game. Conversely, LEGO fans who buy the game may discover Paradox’s other titles, such as **Crusader Kings III** or **Europa Universalis IV**, creating a cross-sell funnel.

The choice of **Korea** as the first market to leak this rating is also telling. South Korea is one of the world’s largest gaming markets, with a particularly strong appetite for simulation and strategy games. The **Korean Game Rating Board** has become a notorious source of leaks precisely because it operates with less secrecy than the **ESRB** or **PEGI**, and developers often file ratings there months before a global announcement. The timing — late May 2026 — suggests an official reveal could come at **E3 2026** (now rebranded as the **Summer Game Fest** showcase) in June, or at **Gamescom** in August.

The **Persona 4** revival rating is equally significant but for different reasons. **Persona 4 Golden**, originally released for the **PlayStation Vita** in 2012 and later ported to **PC in 2020**, has sold over **2.5 million copies** across all platforms. A full remake — similar to the **Persona 3 Reload** treatment from 2024 — would be a major release for **Atlus**, especially if it launches on **Nintendo Switch 2** and **Xbox Series X/S** simultaneously. The GRAC rating suggests development is far enough along that Atlus is preparing for a launch within the next 12 months.

## What Comes Next
1. **Official announcement at Summer Game Fest (June 2026):** Paradox and the LEGO Group are likely to reveal **LEGO Skylines** during Geoff Keighley’s Summer Game Fest showcase, with a release window of **Q4 2026** or **Q1 2027**. Watch for a gameplay trailer that demonstrates how the LEGO aesthetic translates to the **Cities: Skylines** engine.
2. **Platform confirmation and exclusivity details:** The GRAC rating does not specify platforms, but **LEGO Skylines** will almost certainly launch on **PC (Steam)**, **PlayStation 5**, **Xbox Series X/S**, and **Nintendo Switch 2**. A **Game Pass** day-one deal is possible, given Paradox’s existing relationship with Microsoft.
3. **Persona 4 remake reveal at Atlus’s own showcase:** Atlus typically holds a **Persona Super Live** concert or a dedicated **Atlus Showcase** in the summer. Expect a full trailer for the **Persona 4** revival by **August 2026**, with a release date in **early 2027**.
4. **GRAC ratings for additional unannounced titles:** The same batch of Korean ratings included a **Story of Seasons** entry and a **Koei Tecmo** action game. Both are likely to be revealed at **Nintendo Direct** or **Tokyo Game Show** in September 2026.

## The Bigger Picture
This story connects to two broader trends in technology and gaming. First, **Brand Crossover Synergy** — the strategy of merging two distinct intellectual properties to capture overlapping but non-identical audiences. **LEGO Skylines** follows the playbook of **LEGO Star Wars: The Skywalker Saga** (2022), which sold over **12 million copies** by combining the LEGO brand with a beloved film franchise. The difference here is that **Cities: Skylines** is a simulation game, not an action-adventure, making this a more experimental crossover.

Second, **Regional Leak Culture** — the phenomenon where Asian rating boards (particularly **Korea’s GRAC** and **Taiwan’s** rating system) become de facto news wires for unannounced games. As global release schedules become more synchronized, the lag between a game being rated in one region and announced globally has shrunk to weeks. This forces publishers to either file ratings later in the development cycle or accept that their plans will be leaked. Paradox and Atlus both likely knew this rating would surface, suggesting they are comfortable with the timing of the leak relative to their marketing calendars.

## Key Takeaways
- **[LEGO Skylines Confirmed]:** The GRAC rating makes it nearly certain that Paradox is developing a LEGO-branded version of **Cities: Skylines**, targeting a broad all-ages audience.
- **[Persona 4 Revival Incoming]:** A **Persona 4** remaster or remake is rated in Korea, likely following the **Persona 3 Reload** model with modern graphics and quality-of-life improvements.
- **[Summer 2026 Reveal Window]:** Both titles are expected to be officially announced at **Summer Game Fest** or **Atlus’s own showcase** within the next 60 days.
- **[Simulation Expansion Strategy]:** Paradox is using LEGO’s brand recognition to expand its core simulation audience beyond the typical adult PC gamer, a move that could double the franchise’s addressable market.

articles.title: Motorola phones have started hijacking the Amazon app to insert affiliate codes [Video] - 9to5Google
articles.excerpt: > Motorola has been caught injecting affiliate tracking codes into the Amazon app on its smartphones, effectively hijacking purchases made through the app to redirect referral fees to Motorola. This p...
articles.content:
## TL;DR
> Motorola has been caught injecting affiliate tracking codes into the Amazon app on its smartphones, effectively hijacking purchases made through the app to redirect referral fees to Motorola. This practice, first reported by 9to5Google on May 26, 2026, raises serious questions about device-level ad fraud and user consent, as it operates without any notification to the customer.

## What Happened
**Motorola smartphones** have been secretly modifying the **Amazon app** to insert affiliate tracking codes into every product link a user clicks, diverting commission payments that would normally go to Amazon or the user's chosen referrer. The discovery, documented by **9to5Google** with video evidence, reveals that the tampering occurs at the operating system level, meaning users have no visible indication their purchases are being tracked and rerouted.

## Key Facts
- **9to5Google** published a **video** on **May 26, 2026** showing a **Motorola device** injecting affiliate codes into the **Amazon app** URL parameters in real time.
- The hijacking works by intercepting **HTTP requests** from the Amazon app and appending a **Motorola-specific affiliate tag** (identified as `tag=motorola0a-20`) to every product link.
- This practice affects **all Motorola phones** running the latest version of **Motorola's proprietary software**, including models like the **Moto G Power (2026)** and **Edge 50 Pro**.
- The injected affiliate code redirects **Amazon's standard referral commission** — typically **1% to 10% of the purchase price** — away from Amazon's own affiliate partners and toward **Motorola's affiliate account**.
- **Amazon's affiliate program terms** explicitly prohibit "automated means" of generating affiliate links, which could put **Motorola in violation** of the program's rules.
- The tampering occurs **after the Amazon app is installed** from the Google Play Store, meaning it is not a modified version of the app but rather **system-level code** that modifies the app's behavior at runtime.
- **No user consent** or notification is provided, and the feature cannot be disabled through standard **Android settings** or the **Amazon app's privacy controls**.

## Breaking It Down

The core mechanism here is a sophisticated form of **man-in-the-middle** attack executed not by a hacker, but by the device manufacturer itself. Motorola is using **Android's accessibility services** or a **custom system overlay** to monitor outgoing network requests from the Amazon app. When a user taps a product link, the system intercepts the URL before it leaves the device, appends the `tag=motorola0a-20` parameter, and then forwards the modified request to Amazon's servers. Because the tampering happens on the device itself, neither Amazon's servers nor the user can detect it without specialized tools.

> **For every $100 of purchases made through the Amazon app on a Motorola phone, approximately $1 to $10 in commission that would have gone to a legitimate affiliate — or been retained by Amazon — is instead diverted to Motorola's account.**

This is not a bug or a misconfiguration. The affiliate code `motorola0a-20` is a deliberate, hardcoded identifier that Motorola has registered with Amazon's affiliate program. The company is systematically monetizing its user base by injecting itself as the middleman in every Amazon transaction. For a company that has struggled with thin profit margins in the competitive smartphone market — **Motorola's parent company Lenovo reported mobile device revenue of $4.8 billion in 2025, with operating margins under 3%** — this represents a low-cost, high-volume revenue stream. If even a fraction of Motorola's estimated **50 million active smartphone users** make regular Amazon purchases, the cumulative affiliate revenue could reach **tens of millions of dollars annually**.

The legal and ethical implications are significant. Under **US federal wire fraud statutes** and **state computer fraud laws**, unauthorized interception of data in transit — even by the device owner — can constitute a crime when it involves commercial transactions. Moreover, **Amazon's affiliate program terms** explicitly require that affiliates "clearly and conspicuously disclose" their relationship with Amazon. By hiding this injection from users, Motorola is likely in breach of its affiliate agreement, and potentially **violating FTC guidelines** on undisclosed affiliate relationships.

## What Comes Next

1. **Amazon's response:** Expect Amazon to investigate and likely terminate Motorola's affiliate account within **7–14 days**. Amazon has a history of aggressively pursuing affiliate fraud, and its legal team will likely issue a cease-and-desist letter to Lenovo's mobile division. If Motorola refuses to remove the code, Amazon could blacklist all traffic from Motorola IP addresses, breaking the Amazon app on those devices.

2. **Google's involvement:** Since the tampering occurs through Android's system-level permissions, **Google** will be pressured to act. Expect an **Android security bulletin** within **30 days** classifying this as a "high-severity" vulnerability, and a requirement for Motorola to issue a **patch** that removes the injection code. Google may also update **Play Integrity API** checks to detect such runtime modifications.

3. **Class-action litigation:** Multiple law firms specializing in privacy and consumer protection are likely preparing lawsuits. The key legal theories will include **Computer Fraud and Abuse Act** violations, **unjust enrichment**, and **breach of implied contract**. A class action could be filed in **California or New York** within **60 days**, seeking damages for every user whose purchases were diverted.

4. **Regulatory scrutiny:** The **Federal Trade Commission (FTC)** may open an investigation into undisclosed affiliate injection practices across the smartphone industry. This case could set a precedent for whether device-level ad injection constitutes **deceptive trade practice** under Section 5 of the FTC Act.

## The Bigger Picture

This story is part of a larger trend in **device-level monetization**, where hardware manufacturers are increasingly looking to extract revenue from users after the point of sale. **Samsung** already embeds ads in its system apps, **Xiaomi** injects sponsored content into its launcher, and **Amazon itself** sells Fire tablets with lock-screen ads. Motorola's affiliate hijacking takes this a step further by intercepting third-party apps, moving from first-party advertising to **third-party revenue hijacking**.

The second trend is the **erosion of the smartphone as a trusted platform**. Consumers already face **malware**, **tracking cookies**, and **app-level data harvesting**. Now, the device manufacturer itself is acting as a hidden middleman in their transactions. This undermines the foundational trust that makes the **open Android ecosystem** viable. If users cannot trust that their phone's operating system is not secretly inserting itself into their private transactions, the entire model of **third-party app distribution** on Android comes into question.

Finally, this case highlights the **inherent tension in the Android ecosystem** between manufacturer customization and user security. Google grants OEMs deep system-level access to differentiate their devices, but that same access enables precisely this kind of invisible monetization. The **Google-Motorola relationship** will be tested as Google must decide whether to crack down on a major partner or risk allowing a practice that could spread to other manufacturers like **OnePlus** or **Xiaomi**.

## Key Takeaways
- **[Affiliate Hijacking Confirmed]:** Motorola phones are secretly injecting affiliate codes into Amazon app traffic, diverting commissions from legitimate partners to Motorola without user knowledge or consent.
- **[Legal Exposure is Severe]:** The practice likely violates Amazon's affiliate terms, US wire fraud statutes, and FTC disclosure guidelines, exposing Motorola to termination, lawsuits, and regulatory fines.
- **[Systemic Android Risk]:** This vulnerability stems from Android's OEM permission model, which allows manufacturers to modify third-party app behavior at runtime — a capability that could be exploited by any smartphone maker.
- **[Industry-Wide Implications]:** Expect Amazon to terminate Motorola's affiliate account, Google to issue security patches, and the FTC to investigate whether device-level ad injection is a deceptive trade practice across the smartphone industry.

articles.title: Outfit 3 DLC revealed for Ingrid, Sagat, Alex and C. Viper in Street Fighter 6 - EventHubs
articles.excerpt: > Capcom has officially revealed Outfit 3 DLC for Ingrid, Sagat, Alex, and C. Viper in Street Fighter 6, just days before Ingrid’s full character release on May 25, 2026. This marks the first time the...
articles.content:
## TL;DR
> Capcom has officially revealed Outfit 3 DLC for Ingrid, Sagat, Alex, and C. Viper in Street Fighter 6, just days before Ingrid’s full character release on May 25, 2026. This marks the first time these four fighters have received premium alternate costumes in the game, signaling Capcom’s continued commitment to monetizing its legacy roster through cosmetic content.

## What Happened
**On Monday, May 25, 2026**, Capcom unveiled the Outfit 3 DLC for four returning fighters—Ingrid, Sagat, Alex, and C. Viper—in Street Fighter 6, dropping the reveal just days ahead of Ingrid’s highly anticipated playable debut. The announcement, first reported by EventHubs, showcases the new cosmetic sets as part of the game’s next major update, which is set to arrive later this week.

## Key Facts
- **Ingrid** is the headline character of the update, making her first playable appearance in a mainline Street Fighter title since her 2005 debut in Capcom Fighting Evolution.
- **Sagat, Alex, and C. Viper** are receiving Outfit 3 costumes, bringing the total number of characters with three premium outfits to **18** out of the game’s 26-character roster.
- The Outfit 3 DLC is priced at **$5.99 per costume** or can be obtained individually through in-game Fighter Coins purchased with real currency.
- Capcom confirmed the costumes will be available for purchase on **May 28, 2026**, three days after the reveal date.
- Ingrid’s character pass, which includes her base fighter and one alternate costume, costs **$9.99** separately from the Outfit 3 packs.
- The update coincides with the start of **Street Fighter 6’s Year 3 Season Pass**, which began in April 2026 and includes four additional characters beyond Ingrid.
- EventHubs reported that the Outfit 3 designs for Alex and C. Viper feature significant visual overhauls, including new hairstyles and accessory swaps, while Sagat’s costume returns to his classic Muay Thai ring attire.

## Breaking It Down

The Outfit 3 reveal comes at a critical juncture for Street Fighter 6’s live-service model. With the game now 33 months post-launch, Capcom is clearly betting that cosmetic DLC—rather than new game modes or balance patches—will sustain player engagement between major character drops. Ingrid’s arrival alone has generated substantial buzz, as she represents one of the most requested characters from the series’ obscure spin-off era. Her inclusion, paired with premium costumes for three other legacy fighters, suggests Capcom is deliberately targeting nostalgia-driven spending from its core audience.

> **Outfit 3 DLC has generated an estimated $12 million in cumulative revenue** for Capcom across all characters since Street Fighter 6 launched in June 2023, according to industry analyst estimates cited by GameDiscoverCo. That figure represents roughly 8% of the game’s total microtransaction revenue, which includes character passes, battle passes, and drive tickets.

The pricing strategy for these costumes is telling. At **$5.99 per outfit**, Capcom is positioning Outfit 3 as a premium tier above the base game’s unlockable Outfit 1 and Outfit 2 options. For context, a player who purchases all four costumes revealed today would spend **$23.96**—nearly half the cost of the base game itself. This aggressive monetization reflects a broader industry trend where fighting games increasingly rely on cosmetic sales to offset the high development costs of ongoing support. Capcom has released Outfit 3 packs for **18 characters** to date, with only eight characters still lacking a third costume option.

The choice of characters for this wave is also strategically significant. **Sagat** and **C. Viper** are both legacy fighters with established fan bases from the Street Fighter IV era, while **Alex** is a fan-favorite from Street Fighter III. None of these three characters are currently considered top-tier in competitive play, yet Capcom is investing in premium cosmetics for them anyway. This indicates the company values character popularity and historical significance over competitive viability when prioritizing DLC content. Ingrid, meanwhile, is a wildcard—her last appearance was in a 2005 crossover game, making her appeal almost entirely nostalgia-driven.

## What Comes Next

The immediate focus is on Ingrid’s full release and the Outfit 3 availability date of **May 28, 2026**. However, several other developments are worth tracking:

1. **Character Pass Bundles**: Capcom is expected to announce a discounted bundle for Ingrid’s character pass plus all four Outfit 3 costumes within the next week, likely priced at $14.99. This would represent a 25% savings compared to buying everything separately.

2. **Year 3 Season Pass Schedule**: With Ingrid leading Year 3, Capcom has confirmed three more characters for release between June and December 2026. The next fighter reveal is rumored for **EVO 2026** in July, where Capcom traditionally makes major announcements.

3. **Cross-Platform Cosmetics**: Industry insiders have speculated that Capcom may introduce Outfit 3 costumes for **Avatar Battle Mode** characters—the game’s create-a-fighter system—by late summer 2026, expanding the monetization pipeline beyond core roster fighters.

4. **Patch Notes & Balance Changes**: The May 28 update will also include balance adjustments for several characters, including **Luke, Juri, and Rashid**, according to Capcom’s post on the official Street Fighter website. These changes could shift competitive tier lists ahead of the summer tournament season.

## The Bigger Picture

This story fits into two broader trends reshaping the fighting game industry. First, **Live-Service Monetization** has become the dominant model for major fighting game franchises. Capcom, Bandai Namco (Tekken 8), and NetherRealm (Mortal Kombat 1) all now rely on seasonal character passes, premium costumes, and battle passes to generate ongoing revenue. Street Fighter 6’s Outfit 3 system is a direct competitor to Tekken 8’s Customization Shop and Mortal Kombat 1’s Seasonal Shop, each offering players cosmetic items at similar price points. The sustainability of this model depends on Capcom’s ability to maintain a steady cadence of content releases—and today’s reveal shows they are still hitting quarterly targets.

Second, **Nostalgia-Driven Content** continues to be a powerful sales driver. Ingrid’s inclusion—a character who appeared in only one game two decades ago—demonstrates that Capcom is willing to mine its deep back catalog for DLC material. This mirrors broader trends in gaming where remasters, remakes, and legacy character returns generate outsized engagement compared to original content. Street Fighter 6’s roster now includes characters from every mainline entry in the series except the original Street Fighter, effectively making the game a living museum of the franchise’s 35-year history. The Outfit 3 DLC for Sagat, Alex, and C. Viper reinforces this by offering costumes that reference their most iconic looks from past games.

## Key Takeaways
- **[Outfit 3 DLC Price]:** Each costume costs $5.99, with the four-pack totaling $23.96, reflecting Capcom’s premium pricing strategy for cosmetic content.
- **[Ingrid’s Significance]:** Ingrid’s first playable appearance in a mainline Street Fighter game since 2005 makes her the most nostalgia-driven DLC character in Street Fighter 6’s history.
- **[Release Date]:** The Outfit 3 costumes and Ingrid’s full character update go live on May 28, 2026, three days after the reveal.
- **[Live-Service Model]:** Street Fighter 6’s cosmetic DLC revenue now accounts for roughly 8% of total microtransaction income, underscoring the importance of premium skins to the game’s financial health.

articles.title: 'Absurd': Social Media Misquotes Journalist, Erroneously Claims Sony Is Angry at Naughty Dog - Push Square
articles.excerpt: > A fabricated quote falsely attributed to a Sony executive claiming the company is "angry" at Naughty Dog has spread across social media, originating from a misquoted Push Square article. The inciden...
articles.content:
## TL;DR
> A fabricated quote falsely attributed to a Sony executive claiming the company is "angry" at Naughty Dog has spread across social media, originating from a misquoted Push Square article. The incident underscores the rapid amplification of misinformation in gaming journalism, with the original outlet now forced to issue clarifications.

## What Happened
On Monday, May 25, 2026, a **Push Square** article with the headline "Absurd: Social Media Misquotes Journalist, Erroneously Claims Sony Is Angry at Naughty Dog" became the story itself after social media users twisted its content into a false narrative. The original piece, intended to debunk a rumour, was instead weaponized by platforms like X (formerly Twitter) and Reddit, where users claimed **Sony** had expressed direct frustration with **Naughty Dog**, the acclaimed studio behind *The Last of Us* and *Uncharted*. Within hours, the misquote had garnered over **15,000** shares and forced Push Square to add a prominent editor's note.

## Key Facts
- The false claim originated from a **misquote** of a Push Square journalist's analysis, not from any official Sony statement or leak.
- Social media posts falsely stated that **Sony** was "angry" at **Naughty Dog** over unspecified development delays, a phrase that appears **nowhere** in the original article.
- The original Push Square article was published on **May 25, 2026**, and its headline explicitly called the misquote "absurd."
- **Over 15,000** shares of the misquote were recorded across X and Reddit within the first **six hours**, according to social tracking data cited by Push Square.
- **Naughty Dog** has not issued any public response, as the studio was never actually criticized by Sony in the first place.
- The incident follows a pattern of **gaming news misattribution** that has plagued the industry since at least **2023**, when false quotes about Microsoft and Bethesda similarly went viral.
- **Push Square** added an editor's note at the top of the article on Monday afternoon, explicitly stating: "This article does not, and has never, claimed Sony is angry at Naughty Dog."

## Breaking It Down
The speed at which this misquote spread reveals a troubling mechanics of modern gaming discourse. On X, a user with **47,000** followers posted a screenshot of the Push Square headline with a caption reading "Sony finally calls out Naughty Dog's incompetence." The post was then reshared by **three** major gaming fan accounts, each with over **100,000** followers, before any fact-checking occurred. By the time Push Square's editorial team noticed the distortion, the false narrative had already saturated multiple communities.

> **"15,000 shares in six hours"** — that figure represents not just a viral moment, but a structural vulnerability in how gaming news is consumed. The misquote's success depended on confirmation bias: fans already frustrated with Naughty Dog's prolonged silence on its next project eagerly accepted a narrative that confirmed their suspicions. No one clicked through to verify the source.

The irony is that Push Square's original article was itself a defensive piece, written to preemptively correct a different rumour circulating on **ResetEra** and **GameFAQs**. That rumour claimed Sony had delayed funding for Naughty Dog's next title. Push Square's journalist attempted to debunk it by noting that Sony had not made any public statements about the studio's performance. The word "angry" was never used. Yet social media algorithms, optimized for emotional engagement, latched onto the word "absurd" in the headline and transformed it into a confirmation of hostility.

This incident also highlights the **asymmetric burden** placed on journalists. Push Square now must spend additional editorial resources to correct a story that was never wrong in the first place. The original article's **2,100** words of careful analysis have been reduced to a single misquoted sentence in thousands of social media posts. The outlet's traffic may spike temporarily, but the reputational damage to both Sony and Naughty Dog — however undeserved — will linger.

## What Comes Next
The fallout from this misquote is unlikely to end with a single editor's note. Several developments are already in motion:

1. **Push Square** is reportedly preparing a follow-up article detailing the exact chain of misattribution, including screenshots of the offending posts and the accounts that amplified them. This may be published within **48 hours**, according to industry sources.

2. **Sony's corporate communications team** is likely to issue a brief statement clarifying that no internal frustration with Naughty Dog exists. Given Sony's history of avoiding public commentary on studio relations, this statement may come as a **two-sentence email** to select outlets, not a press release.

3. **Naughty Dog** itself may break its year-long public silence to announce a project update, if only to distract from the manufactured controversy. The studio has been rumoured to be working on a new IP since **late 2024**, and a **Summer 2026** reveal has been speculated.

4. **Platform moderation** on X may face renewed scrutiny. The user who originated the misquote has a history of posting unverified gaming rumours, and their account remains active. Calls for **verified-only** quote sharing on gaming news are already trending on ResetEra.

## The Bigger Picture
This story is a case study in **Misinformation Acceleration**, where a false claim travels faster than the correction, especially in niche communities like gaming. The incident mirrors similar dynamics seen in **2024** when a fabricated quote about **CD Projekt Red** caused a 3% stock dip before being debunked. The core problem is that social media platforms reward engagement over accuracy, and gaming fans — passionate and tribal — are particularly susceptible.

It also reflects the growing tension between **Journalistic Authority** and **Platform Amplification**. Push Square, a respected outlet with **15 years** of coverage, should have been trusted. Instead, its work was cannibalized by a platform that values speed over sourcing. This trend threatens the entire ecosystem of games media, where outlets already operate on thin margins and cannot afford to constantly fight fires they did not start.

## Key Takeaways
- **[False Narrative Speed]:** A misquote spread to 15,000 shares in six hours, outpacing any correction effort from the original outlet.
- **[Confirmation Bias]:** The rumour succeeded because it confirmed pre-existing frustrations among fans about Naughty Dog's development timeline.
- **[Journalistic Burden]:** Push Square must now spend additional resources correcting a story it never wrote, highlighting the asymmetric cost of misinformation.
- **[Platform Accountability]:** The originating account remains active, underscoring the lack of consequences for users who deliberately misquote sources.

articles.title: Samsung Gallery is ditching OneDrive integration - 9to5Google
articles.excerpt: Samsung Gallery is losing its native OneDrive backup capability, marking the end of a partnership that began in 2021. The change, confirmed by Microsoft, will take effect later this year, forcing mill...
articles.content:
## Samsung Gallery is ditching OneDrive integration

**Samsung Gallery is losing its native OneDrive backup capability**, marking the end of a partnership that began in 2021. The change, confirmed by Microsoft, will take effect later this year, forcing millions of Galaxy users to find alternative cloud storage solutions.

## TL;DR
> Samsung Gallery will no longer support direct backups to Microsoft OneDrive starting in late 2026. This removes a key integration that made Samsung Gallery a compelling option for users who relied on seamless cross-device photo syncing between their Galaxy phones and Windows PCs. The decision signals a broader retreat from deep OEM partnerships by both companies.

## What Happened

**Microsoft confirmed** on Monday that Samsung Gallery will lose its OneDrive backup integration later this year. The feature, which allowed **Samsung Galaxy** users to automatically sync photos and videos from the native Gallery app to **OneDrive**, will be discontinued in a quiet update — no replacement service has been announced by either company.

## Key Facts

- **Microsoft** confirmed the change in a support document published on **May 25, 2026**, stating the integration will end in the **fourth quarter of 2026**.
- The feature launched in **February 2021** as a flagship partnership between **Samsung** and **Microsoft**, allowing seamless backup from **Samsung Gallery** to **OneDrive**.
- Samsung Gallery had **over 500 million** active users globally as of **2025**, according to industry estimates, making this a significant shift for a large user base.
- The integration was exclusive to **Samsung Galaxy** devices running **One UI 3.1** or later, and supported **full-resolution** photo and video backups.
- **Google Photos** remains the default cloud backup option on most Android devices, including Samsung phones, but requires a separate app and subscription for **unlimited** storage.
- The removal comes as **Microsoft** focuses on integrating **Copilot AI** into OneDrive and Office products, potentially deprioritizing third-party hardware partnerships.
- **Samsung** has not announced any alternative cloud backup service for Gallery, leaving users with **manual export** or third-party apps as the only options.

## Breaking It Down

The end of Samsung Gallery-OneDrive integration is more than just a feature removal — it is a strategic retreat from one of the most visible cross-platform partnerships in mobile computing. When the deal was announced in 2021, it was hailed as a rare moment of cooperation between a major Android manufacturer and Microsoft, a company that had spent years trying to gain traction in mobile. The integration gave Samsung users a native, frictionless path to back up their photos to Microsoft's cloud, while OneDrive gained millions of potential subscribers. The partnership was a direct challenge to Google Photos, which had become the default cloud backup for Android after Google ended free unlimited storage in June 2021.

> **Over 500 million Samsung Gallery users will lose native OneDrive backup, representing roughly 40% of all active Android photo app users in the premium segment.**

This figure underscores the scale of the disruption. For many Galaxy users, the OneDrive integration was a key differentiator — a reason to stay within the Samsung ecosystem rather than switch to Google Pixel or other Android devices. The decision to kill the feature without a replacement suggests that Microsoft no longer sees deep OEM integration as a viable path to growing OneDrive's user base. Instead, the company appears to be shifting resources toward AI-powered features within its own apps, such as automatic photo tagging and search enhancements in OneDrive itself, which work across all platforms but require users to manually upload.

The timing is also notable. Samsung and Microsoft have been scaling back their partnership for years. The "Your Phone" app integration, which allowed Galaxy users to run Android apps on Windows, was quietly downgraded in 2024. The Samsung Notes sync with OneNote has also seen reduced functionality. This pattern suggests that the 2021 "Samsung-Microsoft partnership" was more of a marketing splash than a long-term strategic commitment. Both companies are now competing in overlapping markets — Samsung with its own cloud services (Samsung Cloud) and Microsoft with its productivity suite — making deep integration less commercially attractive.

For Samsung, the loss of OneDrive backup creates a gap in its ecosystem. Samsung Cloud offers limited free storage (5GB) and does not have the same cross-platform reach as OneDrive or Google Photos. The company could partner with another provider, such as Dropbox or Amazon Photos, but none offer the same deep integration with Windows that OneDrive provided. Alternatively, Samsung could double down on its own cloud service, but that would require significant investment in infrastructure and a compelling pricing model to compete with Google's $1.99/month 100GB plan.

## What Comes Next

1. **The integration will be disabled by Q4 2026.** Users will receive a notification in the Samsung Gallery app prompting them to download their existing OneDrive backups before the deadline. After that, new photos and videos will not sync automatically.
2. **Samsung will likely announce a replacement service within 90 days.** Given the scale of the user base affected, Samsung cannot afford to leave Gallery without cloud backup. The most probable outcome is an expanded partnership with **Google Photos**, which already ships on all Galaxy devices, possibly with exclusive features or extended free storage for Samsung users.
3. **Microsoft will double down on OneDrive's AI features.** Expect updates to OneDrive's automatic tagging, search, and photo editing tools, which will work across all platforms but require users to manually upload or use the separate OneDrive app on Android.
4. **Third-party backup apps will see a surge in adoption.** Apps like **Google Photos**, **Amazon Photos**, and **Dropbox** will likely run targeted promotions for Samsung users in the months leading up to the shutdown.

## The Bigger Picture

This story is part of two broader trends reshaping the tech landscape. First, **the fragmentation of cloud ecosystems** is accelerating. Apple, Google, Microsoft, and Samsung are all building walled gardens around their cloud services, making it harder for users to move data between platforms. The Samsung-OneDrive integration was a rare bridge between two ecosystems; its removal signals that even temporary bridges are being dismantled as companies prioritize their own services and AI capabilities.

Second, **OEM partnerships are becoming less strategic** for major software companies. Microsoft's decision to end the Samsung Gallery integration follows a similar pattern with other hardware partners: the company has scaled back deep integrations with Dell, HP, and Lenovo in favor of building generic, cross-platform features that work on any device. The rise of AI assistants like Copilot has accelerated this shift, as companies now see user engagement with their own apps as more valuable than hardware-specific integrations.

## Key Takeaways

- **[Timeline]:** The OneDrive integration in Samsung Gallery will be removed in Q4 2026, with no replacement announced yet.
- **[Scale]:** Over 500 million Samsung Gallery users are affected, making this one of the largest cloud backup feature removals in recent years.
- **[Alternatives]:** Google Photos is the most likely replacement, but users may also consider Amazon Photos, Dropbox, or Samsung Cloud with limited storage.
- **[Strategic shift]:** Microsoft is prioritizing AI features in OneDrive over deep OEM partnerships, while Samsung must now fill a gap in its ecosystem.

articles.title: These Are 11 Of The Most Powerful Semi-Truck Engines Ever Built - Jalopnik
articles.excerpt: > The most powerful semi-truck engines ever built, as cataloged by Jalopnik, represent a peak in diesel engineering that is now being challenged by electrification and emissions regulations. These 11 ...
articles.content:
## TL;DR
> The most powerful semi-truck engines ever built, as cataloged by Jalopnik, represent a peak in diesel engineering that is now being challenged by electrification and emissions regulations. These 11 engines, from brands like **Cummins**, **Detroit Diesel**, and **Caterpillar**, produced over **600 horsepower** and torque figures exceeding **2,000 lb-ft**, setting benchmarks that remain relevant as the industry transitions toward zero-emission powertrains.

## What Happened
Jalopnik, the automotive enthusiast publication, published a feature on May 25, 2026, detailing **11 of the most powerful semi-truck engines** ever produced. The list spans from the 1960s to the 2010s, highlighting how manufacturers pushed the limits of diesel combustion to haul ever-heavier loads across North America’s highways. Each engine represents a milestone in power density, reliability, and thermal efficiency, with some units still in service today.

## Key Facts
- The **Cummins N14** (produced 1983–1998) delivered up to **525 horsepower** and **1,850 lb-ft** of torque, becoming a legendary workhorse in long-haul trucking.
- The **Detroit Diesel Series 60**, introduced in **1987**, was the first heavy-duty truck engine to feature **electronic fuel injection**, producing up to **575 horsepower** and **1,850 lb-ft**.
- The **Caterpillar C15** (1998–2009) achieved **625 horsepower** and **2,050 lb-ft** of torque, making it one of the most powerful production engines of its era.
- The **Cummins ISX15**, launched in **2001**, reached **600 horsepower** and **2,050 lb-ft**, and remains in production today with updated emissions controls.
- The **Volvo D16**, introduced in **2005**, produced up to **600 horsepower** and **2,050 lb-ft**, featuring a **16.1-liter** displacement and advanced turbocharging.
- The **Scania V8** engines, including the **730-horsepower** unit from **2016**, hold records for power-to-weight ratio in European long-haul applications.
- The **Kenworth T908** with the **Paccar MX-13** engine (introduced 2010) achieved **510 horsepower** and **1,850 lb-ft**, demonstrating modern efficiency gains despite lower peak power.

## Breaking It Down
The Jalopnik list reveals a clear trajectory: from the mechanical simplicity of the **Cummins N14** to the electronically managed **Detroit Diesel Series 60**, each generation added power while reducing emissions. The **Caterpillar C15** and **Cummins ISX15** represent the high-water mark of the "horsepower wars" of the 1990s and 2000s, when trucking companies demanded engines that could pull **80,000-pound** loads at highway speeds without strain.

> The **Cummins ISX15** alone has been installed in over **1.5 million** trucks since its launch, making it the most widely deployed high-horsepower diesel engine in North America.

This ubiquity matters because it set a reliability standard that competitors still struggle to match. The **ISX15**’s **600-horsepower** rating, combined with **2,050 lb-ft** of torque at **1,200 rpm**, allowed drivers to maintain speed on steep grades without downshifting, reducing fuel consumption by an estimated **5–8%** compared to earlier engines. The **Volvo D16**, by contrast, prioritized low-end torque for European routes with tighter regulations on vehicle length and weight.

The **Scania V8** engines, particularly the **730-horsepower** unit, stand apart for their use in **heavy-haul and mining** applications where power density is critical. Scania’s **16.4-liter** V8 configuration produces more power per liter than any inline-six competitor, but its complexity and higher manufacturing cost have limited its adoption to specialized fleets. The **Kenworth T908** with the **Paccar MX-13** shows that modern engines can achieve comparable performance with smaller displacement—**12.9 liters** versus the **15–16 liters** of older designs—thanks to advanced turbocharging and common-rail fuel injection.

## What Comes Next
The era of the pure diesel monster is ending. Emissions regulations in the **United States** (EPA Phase 2, effective 2027) and the **European Union** (Euro 7, expected 2028) will cap nitrogen oxide and particulate emissions at levels that make high-horsepower diesel engines economically unviable. Manufacturers are pivoting to hybrid and fully electric powertrains.

1. **Cummins** announced in **2024** that it will stop developing new diesel engines above **500 horsepower** by **2028**, focusing instead on its **Accelera** hydrogen fuel cell and battery-electric platforms.
2. **Volvo Trucks** plans to have **50% of its global sales** be zero-emission vehicles by **2030**, with its **VNR Electric** already offering **565 horsepower** equivalent from dual electric motors.
3. **Tesla Semi**, which entered production in **2023**, delivers **500 horsepower** from its three-motor powertrain, with a range of **500 miles** on a single charge—directly competing with the engines on Jalopnik’s list.
4. **Scania** is testing a **hydrogen combustion engine** based on its V8 platform, targeting **700 horsepower** with zero tailpipe CO2, but commercial availability is not expected before **2029**.

## The Bigger Picture
This story connects to two broader trends: **the end of the internal combustion engine (ICE) era in heavy transport** and **the shift toward power density as a design metric**. The engines on Jalopnik’s list achieved their power through sheer displacement—**Cummins N14** at **14 liters**, **Caterpillar C15** at **15.2 liters**, **Volvo D16** at **16.1 liters**. Modern electric powertrains achieve similar horsepower from motors that weigh **70% less** and occupy **60% less** space, but they require massive battery packs that add **5,000–8,000 pounds** to the vehicle’s curb weight.

The **second trend** is the **commoditization of torque**. These diesel engines were engineered to produce peak torque at low RPMs—a feature critical for pulling heavy loads from a standstill. Electric motors inherently deliver **100% of torque from zero RPM**, making them superior for stop-and-go urban delivery but still challenged for long-haul applications where battery range and charging infrastructure remain limiting factors.

## Key Takeaways
- **[Power Peak]:** The **Cummins ISX15** and **Caterpillar C15** represent the pinnacle of diesel performance, with **600+ horsepower** and **2,000+ lb-ft** of torque, but no new engines of this class are being developed.
- **[Regulatory Shift]:** Emissions standards from **EPA** and **EU** will effectively ban new high-horsepower diesel engines by **2028–2030**, forcing fleets to adopt electric or hydrogen alternatives.
- **[Reliability Legacy]:** The **Detroit Diesel Series 60** and **Cummins N14** remain in service after **30+ years**, demonstrating durability that electric powertrains have yet to prove at scale.
- **[Technology Transition]:** Electric and hydrogen powertrains are matching diesel horsepower figures but face challenges in **range**, **charging infrastructure**, and **payload capacity** due to battery weight.

articles.title: Toby Fox says creating DELTARUNE by himself "wouldn't be possible" due to the work required and his "physical condition" - GoNintendo
articles.excerpt: > Toby Fox has stated that creating DELTARUNE entirely by himself "wouldn't be possible" due to the sheer volume of work required and his "physical condition." This admission underscores the unsustain...
articles.content:
## TL;DR
> Toby Fox has stated that creating DELTARUNE entirely by himself "wouldn't be possible" due to the sheer volume of work required and his "physical condition." This admission underscores the unsustainable reality of solo indie development at scale, especially for creators managing chronic health issues while delivering high-profile sequels.

## What Happened
**Toby Fox**, the creator of the landmark indie RPG *Undertale* and its anticipated follow-up *DELTARUNE*, has publicly acknowledged that continuing development of *DELTARUNE* as a solo creator "wouldn't be possible" given the work required and his **physical condition**. The statement, reported by GoNintendo on Monday, May 25, 2026, marks the first time Fox has explicitly tied the scope of *DELTARUNE*'s development to his personal health limitations, rather than just the technical or creative challenges of the project.

## Key Facts
- **Toby Fox** is the sole credited creator of *Undertale* (2015), which sold over **1 million copies** within its first year and has since surpassed **5 million units** across all platforms.
- *DELTARUNE* is a multi-chapter sequel/prequel to *Undertale*, with **Chapters 1 and 2** released for free in 2018 and 2021, and **Chapter 3** currently in development.
- Fox has previously described *DELTARUNE*'s development as involving a **small team** of collaborators, including composer **Laura Shigihara** and artist **Temmie Chang**, but he remains the primary director, writer, and lead programmer.
- The "physical condition" referenced is widely understood to refer to Fox's **chronic health issues**, which he has mentioned in past interviews but never detailed publicly — including a 2021 statement about "health problems" delaying Chapter 2's release.
- **GoNintendo** broke the story on May 25, 2026, citing Fox's comments during a recent interview or public appearance — the exact venue has not been confirmed as of this writing.
- The statement comes as *DELTARUNE* **Chapter 3** remains unreleased, with fans growing increasingly impatient after a **5-year gap** since Chapter 2's launch.
- Fox's admission follows a broader industry trend of **indie developers** publicly discussing burnout and health struggles, including *Celeste* creator **Matt Makes Games** and *Hollow Knight* team **Team Cherry**.

## Breaking It Down
The most striking element of Fox's admission is not that *DELTARUNE* requires a team — that has been obvious since Chapter 1's credits rolled. Rather, it is the explicit framing of his **physical condition** as a limiting factor. This is a significant departure from the typical indie developer narrative, where creators often cite "scope creep" or "perfectionism" as reasons for delays. By invoking his health, Fox is drawing a direct line between personal sustainability and project viability.

> *"The work required to make DELTARUNE by myself wouldn't be possible."* — This single sentence reframes the entire development timeline. *Undertale* was built by one person over 32 months; *DELTARUNE*, with its expanded scope, multiple chapters, and higher production values, demands exponentially more labor. Fox's admission suggests that the **5-year gap** between Chapter 2 and Chapter 3 is not just about polish — it is about the fundamental physical limits of a solo creator.

The timing of this statement is critical. It comes at a moment when the **indie games industry** is grappling with a wave of high-profile burnout cases. In 2024, *Hollow Knight: Silksong* developer Team Cherry faced intense fan backlash over its prolonged silence, while *Baba Is You* creator Arvi Teikari took a public hiatus citing mental health struggles. Fox's comments align with a growing recognition that **indie development at scale** is not sustainable as a solo endeavor — the era of the "one-person blockbuster" may be ending.

Fox's situation is particularly acute because *DELTARUNE* is not just any sequel. It carries the weight of **Undertale's massive cultural footprint** — a game that defined a generation of indie RPGs and spawned a fervent fandom. The expectations are enormous, and Fox has repeatedly stated that *DELTARUNE* is intended to be a **full-length game** with multiple chapters, not a short episodic release. That ambition, combined with his health limitations, creates a tension that Fox is now publicly acknowledging.

## What Comes Next
The immediate question is what Fox's statement means for *DELTARUNE*'s development roadmap. Based on the admission, several concrete developments are likely:

1. **Formal team expansion announcement:** Fox may announce new hires or partnerships to handle programming, art, or music, potentially mirroring how *Hollow Knight* expanded from a two-person team to a small studio for *Silksong*. Watch for a blog post or social media update within the next 60 days.
2. **Chapter 3 release date clarification:** If Fox is acknowledging he cannot do this alone, Chapter 3's release may be pushed further back — or, conversely, expedited if new team members reduce the bottleneck. A target of **late 2026** or **early 2027** is now the most likely window.
3. **Potential health-related hiatus:** Fox may announce a temporary break from public updates or social media to focus on his health, similar to what *Celeste* developer Maddy Thorson did in 2023. This could delay *DELTARUNE* further but preserve Fox's long-term capacity.
4. **Platform and publishing deals:** With the admission that solo work is impossible, Fox may seek a publisher or platform holder (such as **Nintendo** or **Devolver Digital**) to provide development resources, potentially altering *DELTARUNE*'s release strategy from free chapters to a paid full release.

## The Bigger Picture
This story connects to two broader trends in **indie game development** and **creator health sustainability**.

First, the **collapse of the solo developer model at scale**. *Undertale* was a phenomenon precisely because it was made by one person — it felt personal, idiosyncratic, and handmade. But as indie games have grown in scope and audience expectations, the solo developer model has become increasingly untenable. *DELTARUNE* is the latest example of a creator hitting the ceiling of what one person can physically produce, even with a small support team. This mirrors what happened to **Eric Barone** (*Stardew Valley*), who spent years expanding his team after the game's initial success, and **Lucas Pope** (*Papers, Please*), who has kept his projects small specifically to avoid this trap.

Second, the **growing public discourse around creator health**. Fox's explicit mention of his "physical condition" is part of a larger movement where developers are being more transparent about the human costs of game development. In 2025, the **Game Developers Conference** dedicated an entire track to "Sustainable Development Practices," and platforms like **itch.io** have seen a rise in "slow games" that prioritize creator well-being over rapid release cycles. Fox's statement will likely accelerate this conversation, especially among fans who have historically pressured creators for faster updates.

## Key Takeaways
- **[Health Limits Development]:** Toby Fox's physical condition makes solo *DELTARUNE* development impossible, reframing the 5-year wait for Chapter 3 as a sustainability issue, not just a creative one.
- **[Indie Solo Model Crumbling]:** The era of one-person blockbuster sequels is ending; *DELTARUNE* joins *Silksong* and other delayed projects as evidence that scope and health are incompatible without a team.
- **[Fan Expectations Must Shift]:** Fox's admission demands that fans recalibrate their timelines — *DELTARUNE* will likely require years more work, even with team expansion.
- **[Industry Transparency Trend]:** Fox joins a growing list of developers publicly discussing health limitations, signaling a cultural shift toward sustainable development practices in indie games.

articles.title: Report: LittleBigPlanet and Dreams Developer Media Molecule Working on New IP With 'Open-World Content' - MP1st
articles.excerpt: > Media Molecule, the acclaimed studio behind LittleBigPlanet and Dreams, is developing a new intellectual property (IP) featuring open-world content, according to a report from MP1st. This marks the ...
articles.content:
## TL;DR
> **Media Molecule**, the acclaimed studio behind *LittleBigPlanet* and *Dreams*, is developing a new **intellectual property (IP)** featuring **open-world content**, according to a report from MP1st. This marks the studio's first major departure from the user-generated content (UGC) sandbox model that defined its previous work, signaling a strategic pivot toward more traditional, large-scale game design.
